name: Indonesia
tagline: 17,000 islands of culture, spice, and ceremony

arrivalSteps.title: Visa / Visa on Arrival (VOA) Preparation
arrivalSteps.description: Citizens of 90+ countries are eligible for the e-VOA paid online at molina.imigrasi.go.id before travel, or at the on-arrival counter at major airports. Cost: IDR 500,000 (approx. USD 30) for a 30-day stay, extendable once. Pre-purchase online to avoid airport queues. Some nationalities receive free visa-free access for 30 days.

arrivalSteps.title: Health & Customs e-Declaration
arrivalSteps.description: Complete the Electronic Customs Declaration (CD) via the Indonesian Customs app (Bea Cukai) or the paper form on arrival. At major airports, this is mandated before reaching the baggage carousel.

arrivalSteps.title: Passport Control / Immigration Counter
arrivalSteps.description: Present your passport and VOA payment receipt (or visa) at the immigration counter. Officers will verify your documents, take a facial photograph, and stamp your passport. Confirm your intended address and duration of stay.

arrivalSteps.title: Biometrics
arrivalSteps.description: Fingerprint scans (both index fingers) and a facial photograph are taken at the immigration counter for all foreign nationals. This is mandatory under Indonesian immigration law.

arrivalSteps.title: Baggage Claim
arrivalSteps.description: Collect luggage from the designated carousel. Retain baggage receipts as they are checked at the exit. Random luggage scans are common at Bali and Jakarta airports.

arrivalSteps.title: Customs Declaration
arrivalSteps.description: Duty-free: goods up to USD 500 per person, 200 cigarettes, 25 cigars or 100g tobacco, 1L alcohol. Declare cash over USD 10,000. Strictly prohibited: narcotics (death penalty enforced), pornographic material, and agricultural products without a phytosanitary certificate.

fallbackAlerts.message: Indonesia enforces the death penalty for drug trafficking. Do not accept packages from strangers.
Importing pornographic material into Indonesia is illegal and can result in criminal charges.

scenarios.description: Balinese Hindu temple etiquette and dress requirements

arrivalSteps.title: Wear a Sarong and Sash
arrivalSteps.description: A sarong and a sash (selendang) must be worn to enter any Balinese Hindu temple. They are available to rent at the entrance.

arrivalSteps.title: Women on Menstruation Must Not Enter
arrivalSteps.description: By Balinese Hindu custom, women who are menstruating should not enter the inner temple. Signs are posted.

arrivalSteps.title: Never Point Your Feet at Shrines
arrivalSteps.description: Sit with legs folded away from shrines and offerings. Pointing feet at sacred objects is deeply offensive.

fallbackAlerts.message: A sarong and sash are mandatory to enter Balinese Hindu temples. They can be rented at the gate for a small fee.
Do not step over or kick temple offerings (canang sari) placed on the ground — they are sacred.

phrases.english: Where can I rent a sarong?
phrases.nativeScript: Di mana saya bisa menyewa sarung?
phrases.romanized: Di mana saya bisa menyewa sarung?

scenarios.description: Mosque etiquette in the world's largest Muslim-majority country

arrivalSteps.title: Cover Up Completely
arrivalSteps.description: Women must cover their hair, arms, and legs. Men must cover from navel to knees at minimum.

arrivalSteps.title: Remove Shoes
arrivalSteps.description: Remove all footwear before entering the prayer hall.

arrivalSteps.title: Do Not Disturb Friday Prayers
arrivalSteps.description: Friday midday (Jumah) prayer is obligatory for Muslim men. Avoid entering mosques during this time unless invited.

fallbackAlerts.message: During Ramadan, eating, drinking, or smoking in public during daylight hours is disrespectful and illegal in some regions.

phrases.english: Is the mosque open to visitors?
phrases.nativeScript: Apakah masjid ini terbuka untuk pengunjung?
phrases.romanized: Apakah masjid ini terbuka untuk pengunjung?

scenarios.description: Beach laws, surf safety, and coral reef protection

arrivalSteps.title: No Touching Coral
arrivalSteps.description: Touching, breaking, or removing coral is illegal under Indonesian environmental law.

arrivalSteps.title: Public Displays of Affection
arrivalSteps.description: Kissing or embracing in public on beaches can cause offence. Be modest in non-resort areas.

fallbackAlerts.message: Damaging or removing coral reef is illegal in Indonesia and carries heavy fines.

phrases.english: Is it safe to swim here?
phrases.nativeScript: Apakah aman untuk berenang di sini?
phrases.romanized: Apakah aman untuk berenang di sini?
